figures.caption: Figure 13: FashionMNIST: VIB (Gaussian, R16) vs. CEB (Dirichlet, P10). Top-left: error rate vs. β. Top-right: information plane I(W ;Y ) vs. I(X;W ) (CEB only). Bottom-left: rate–distortion curve I(X;Y |W ) vs. I(X;W ) (CEB only). Bottom-right: distortion ε = I(X;Y |W ) vs. β (CEB only). CEB achieves higher accuracy at lower rate with no parametric assumptions beyond the dimension of W .
Figure 14: CEB ablation over simplex dimension K at β = 25. Accuracy saturates at K=|Y|=10. Further increasing K reduces distortion but does not improve classification.
Figure 1: Overview from left to right. (a) The IB optimal encoder. (b) Soft clustering in the probability simplex. (c) Max entropy for less supervised settings.
Figure 8: Continuous experiment: Dirichlet means (blue) vs. ground-truth p(Y |x) (black circles) for increasing β.
Figure 9: Continuous experiment: Information Plane, Rate–Distortion curve, and ε(β).
Figure 3: Example (26) inside P3. Blue points: M = {w1, w2, w3}, the predictive manifold. Dashed triangle: convex hull of M (visual aid). Grey dot: marginal p(Y ) = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3).

Figure 5: Tangent to the IB curve at operating point (R0,∆ ∗(R0)). The slope of the tangent is 1/β and its ∆-axis intercept is c(R0) = ∆∗(R0) − R0/β. As R0 increases, β grows, the slope flattens, and c(R0) rises from 0 to I(X;Y ).
Figure 11: Discrete CEB experiment: Dirichlet means vs. ground-truth for increasing β.

Figure 4: The achievable region (light blue trapezoid) in the (I(X;W ), I(W ;Y )) plane, bounded by the converse boundary C (gray dashed: diagonal and horizontal data processing inequality bounds), the R-axis, and R = H(X). The blue line is the time-sharing lower bound on the IB curve. The darker shaded triangle is the gap region between the converse boundary and the time-sharing line. The true IB curve (red) is concave, connects (0, 0) to (H(W ∗), I(X;Y )), and is flat at I(X;Y ) for R ∈ [H(W ∗), H(X)]. When H(W ∗|Y ) = 0, the gap triangle collapses and the IB curve coincides with the converse boundary up to R = I(X;Y ) = H(W ∗).
Figure 12: Discrete CEB experiment: Information Plane, Rate–Distortion curve, and ε(β). Dashed lines mark H(X) and H(W ∗).

Figure 2: Soft clustering of the predictive manifold M ⊂ P3 at three distortion levels. Each triangle is the probability simplex P3 with vertices e1, e2, e3. The red curve is M = {p(Y |x) : x ∈ X}. Blue dots are individual predictive distributions p(Y |xi). (a) At ε = 0, the encoder computes W ∗ = p(Y |X) exactly (see Theorem 1). (b) At moderate ε, nearby points merge into soft clusters. Black dots are cluster centers p(y|w) = E[W ∗|W=w] (23). (c) As ε → I(X;Y ), all points merge into a single cluster centered at the marginal p(y), and W ⊥ X.
